title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?
hints: Think about how the number of ways to reach step n relates to previous steps.
To reach step n, you either came from step n-1 or step n-2. This looks like a famous sequence.
It's the Fibonacci sequence! dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2], with dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2.
follow_ups: What if you can climb 1, 2, or 3 steps?
Can you solve it with matrix exponentiation in O(log n)?

title: Coin Change
description: You are given coins of different denominations and a total amount. Return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. If it's not possible, return -1. You have infinite supply of each coin.
hints: For each amount, you have a choice — which coin to use. This gives you overlapping subproblems.
Define dp[i] = minimum coins needed for amount i. For each coin, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1).
Initialize dp[0]=0, everything else to infinity. For each amount from 1 to target, try each coin and take the minimum.
follow_ups: What if you need to count the number of ways instead?
What if each coin can only be used once?

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence doesn't have to be contiguous.
hints: For each element, the LIS ending at that element depends on previous elements. This is a classic DP problem.
dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each j < i, if nums[j] < nums[i], dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).
Initialize all dp[i] = 1. Double loop: for each i, check all j < i. Answer is max of all dp values. O(n^2) solution.
follow_ups: Can you solve it in O(n log n) using binary search?
How would you reconstruct the actual subsequence?

title: Longest Common Subsequence
description: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some or no elements without changing the order.
hints: Compare characters at each position in both strings. What are the two cases?
If characters match, LCS includes both. If not, try skipping one character from either string.
dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[0..i-1] and text2[0..j-1]. If chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
follow_ups: Can you optimize space to O(min(m,n))?
How would you print the actual LCS?

title: 0/1 Knapsack
description: Given n items with weights and values, and a knapsack capacity W, find the maximum total value you can carry. Each item can be taken at most once.
hints: For each item, you have two choices: take it or leave it. This gives you a recursive structure.
dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. Either skip item i or include it (if it fits).
dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Can be optimized to 1D array iterating backwards.
follow_ups: What if items have unlimited supply (unbounded knapsack)?
Can you reconstruct which items to take?

title: Edit Distance
description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. You can insert, delete, or replace a character.
hints: Compare characters at each position. If they match, no operation needed. If not, you have three choices.
Insert, delete, or replace — each corresponds to moving in different directions in a 2D DP table.
dp[i][j] = edit distance for word1[0..i-1] and word2[0..j-1]. If chars match, dp[i-1][j-1]. Else 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]).
follow_ups: Can you optimize space to O(min(m,n))?
What if the cost of each operation is different?
